title: SEGMENT 15 — Clinical management — ILD, ocular toxicity, neutropenia

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Clinical management — ILD, ocular toxicity, neutropenia
Now let's get clinical for a segment.
Because however elegant the chemistry, the drugs have to be tolerable for patients to actually benefit from them.
And ADCs have characteristic toxicity profiles — not generic chemotherapy toxicities, but specific patterns driven by each drug's design.
The oncologist using these drugs has to understand the toxicity signatures and manage them proactively.
The toxicity that has gotten the most attention in recent years is interstitial lung disease, or ILD, associated with T-DXd.
Roughly ten to fifteen percent of patients treated with T-DXd develop some form of ILD — meaning inflammation and scarring in the lung tissue.
In most cases, it's mild — graded one or two on the standard five-point scale.
But a small percentage of patients develop severe ILD, which can be life-threatening or fatal.
In the original T-DXd trials, there were deaths from ILD, and the FDA labeling carries a black-box warning.
Here's the analogy I want you to hold.
ILD is the lung-leak toxicity — the price T-DXd pays for its bystander effect.
Remember, the bystander effect works because the DXd payload, once released, can diffuse to neighboring cells regardless of antigen status.
That's great in a tumor, where the neighbors are also cancer.
But the lung is full of rapidly dividing cells that may pick up trace amounts of circulating ADC or free payload, and over time, the cumulative damage manifests as inflammation.
The same property that gives T-DXd its clinical superpower against HER2-low disease is the same property that drives lung toxicity.
Clinical management of ILD involves vigilance.
Patients on T-DXd are monitored with chest imaging on a regular schedule, and any new respiratory symptoms — cough, shortness of breath, fatigue — trigger a workup.
If ILD is detected, the response depends on grade.
Grade 1 — asymptomatic, found only on imaging — often allows continuation with closer monitoring.
Grade 2 and above — symptomatic — typically requires permanent drug discontinuation and treatment with corticosteroids.
The decision to stop is consequential, because for many patients T-DXd is the most effective therapy they have access to.
But the risk of progression to fatal ILD is real, and the threshold for stopping is appropriately low.
A second important toxicity is ocular toxicity, particularly seen with the MMAF-containing ADCs like belantamab mafodotin.
MMAF accumulates in the corneal epithelium and causes microcystic changes that can blur vision and, in some patients, cause significant visual loss.
Belantamab mafodotin was actually withdrawn from the U.
S. market in 2022, then reapproved with modifications, partly because of the ocular toxicity profile.
Patients on MMAF ADCs require ophthalmologic monitoring, and dose modifications are often required when corneal changes are detected.
A third category is neutropenia and diarrhea, particularly with topo I-payload ADCs like Trodelvy.
Severe neutropenia — low neutrophil counts — increases infection risk and may require growth factor support with G-CSF.
Severe diarrhea from SN-38 leakage may require aggressive antidiarrheal therapy and dose reduction.
A fourth category is hepatic toxicity, seen with multiple ADC classes but particularly with calicheamicin-based drugs like Mylotarg.
Veno-occlusive disease of the liver is a serious complication, and Mylotarg's labeling carries warnings about it.
The general principle is that ADCs are not free lunches.

They are dramatically more selective than free chemotherapy in some respects, but they have their own characteristic toxicities driven by the payload class, the linker stability, and the specific tissues where antigen happens to be expressed at low levels.
The oncologist using an ADC has to know its specific toxicity profile and monitor for the relevant complications.
ILD for T-DXd.
Ocular toxicity for MMAF.
Diarrhea and neutropenia for topo I ADCs.
Hepatic disease for calicheamicin ADCs.
And as the field expands, new payload classes will introduce new toxicities.
This is, in some sense, unavoidable.
Every payload has off-target effects, and every linker chemistry has imperfect stability, and every antigen has at least some normal-tissue expression.
The art is in matching the toxicity profile to the clinical situation and managing the risks appropriately.
